summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on finally purchasing a Garmin Nuvi GPS device after prices dropped dramatically from $500+ to under $200. He describes his previous workaround of using a laptop with external USB GPS, acknowledging it was unwieldy and sometimes dangerous. After two weeks of use, he enthusiastically endorses the Nuvi as offering an outstanding consumer experience comparable to the original TiVo. He argues that GPS devices have transitioned from high-end geeky toys to practical, affordable technology that everyone should own.
key_points: GPS devices have undergone a dramatic price crash, making them accessible to mainstream consumers
The Garmin Nuvi represents a breakthrough in user experience design for GPS products, prioritizing the complete consumer experience over just technical functionality
People will use suboptimal solutions (like laptops for navigation) to save money, even when those solutions are dangerous or inconvenient
Products that 'just work' can completely change user behavior and break old habits
The transition from geek toy to mainstream essential follows a similar pattern to TiVo and DVRs
money_quotes: After using it for about two weeks, I feel like an idiot for waiting so long.
I'd rate it up there with the original Tivo.
Everything about it just works, to the point that it completely breaks you of the old ways of navigation.
I used to think of GPS devices as high-end geeky toys.
People – myself included – will do crazy things to save a buck.
I'm what you might call an indoor enthusiast.
And I strongly suspect that once you have a Nuvi, like me, you'll wonder how anyone ever lived without it.
key_insight: When technology becomes affordable and user-friendly enough, it transitions from niche geek toy to essential mainstream tool that makes people wonder how they ever lived without it.
